title: Rockwell Automation Logix PLC Hardcoded Cryptographic Key Authentication Bypass CISA KEV: Unauthenticated Attackers Impersonate Engineering Workstations to Modify Industrial Control System Logic (CVE-2021-22681)
affected_products: Rockwell Automation Studio 5000 Logix Designer Versions 21 and later

sources.name: The Hacker News - Hikvision and Rockwell Automation CVSS 9.8 Flaws Added to CISA KEV Catalog
CISA Alert - CISA Adds Five Known Exploited Vulnerabilities to Catalog
Claroty Team82 - Critical Authentication Bypass in Rockwell Software
Vulert Blog - Hikvision and Rockwell Automation CVSS 9.8 Flaws
SOCRadar - CISA Flags Hikvision Camera and Rockwell Logix Vulnerabilities as Actively Exploited
Help Net Security - Critical flaw in Rockwell PLCs allows attackers to fiddle with them
SecurityWeek - Rockwell Vulnerability Allowing Remote ICS Hacking Exploited in Attacks

timeline.event: Claroty Team82 privately discloses the hardcoded cryptographic key vulnerability to Rockwell Automation
Soonchunhyang University (South Korea) and Kaspersky independently discover and report the same vulnerability
Rockwell Automation publicly discloses CVE-2021-22681 and issues Security Advisory PN1550; no patch released — only mitigations available
Claroty Team82 publishes detailed technical research; media coverage confirms authentication bypass enables full PLC compromise
CISA adds CVE-2021-22681 to Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog confirming active in-the-wild exploitation; federal deadline set for March 26, 2026

simple_summary.what_happened: A critical vulnerability in Rockwell Automation industrial controllers (PLCs) was confirmed as actively exploited and added to CISA's must-patch list on March 5, 2026. The flaw stems from a hardcoded cryptographic key embedded in the engineering software (Studio 5000 Logix Designer) that is used to verify communication with the factory controllers. Because this key is insufficiently protected, attackers who obtain it can trick any Rockwell PLC into thinking they are an authorized engineer — allowing them to rewrite the industrial code running physical equipment.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Any organization using Rockwell Automation Logix PLCs in industrial environments: manufacturing plants, energy facilities, water treatment systems, chemical plants, oil and gas operations, and critical infrastructure. Affected products span the full CompactLogix, ControlLogix, DriveLogix, and GuardLogix product lines. Approximately 6,000 Rockwell devices are currently exposed to the internet.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: The attacker obtains the hardcoded cryptographic key by extracting it from Studio 5000 Logix Designer software or through memory analysis of a networked controller. Step 2: The attacker scans the target network for Rockwell Automation controllers using EtherNet/IP protocol on ports 2222 (TCP/UDP). Step 3: The attacker crafts digitally signed packets using the obtained cryptographic key that mimic a legitimate engineering workstation. Step 4: The PLC controller receives the signed packets, verifies the signature using the hardcoded key, and grants full authenticated access — believing it is communicating with an authorized engineer. Step 5: With full access, the attacker uploads modified control logic, changes critical process parameters (setpoints, timers, safety thresholds), installs malicious firmware, or causes physical disruption to connected industrial machinery.
what_you_should_do: Immediately implement network segmentation to isolate all Logix PLC controllers from internet access and untrusted network segments
Enable CIP Security (TLS/DTLS-based encryption) on all Logix controllers to replace the vulnerable hardcoded key mechanism
Set all controllers to 'Run' mode (vs. 'Program' mode) when not actively programming to limit unauthorized code uploads
Enable controller change logging (Logix v17+) and 'Change Detection' feature (Logix v20+) to detect unauthorized modifications
Audit network access to all engineering workstations running Studio 5000 / RSLogix 5000 and implement MFA for remote access
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: There is no patch available for this vulnerability — Rockwell has never released a code fix. Attackers with network access to a PLC can fully compromise it without any credentials, modifying the logic that controls physical industrial processes. In worst-case scenarios this could cause equipment damage, production outages, safety system failures, or physical harm to workers and surrounding communities. The CISA KEV addition on March 5, 2026 confirms this is now being actively weaponized against production systems.

tactics.description: Attackers exploit the insufficiently protected cryptographic key to authenticate to internet-exposed or network-accessible Rockwell Logix PLCs without valid credentials
After gaining authenticated PLC access, attackers can install malicious firmware into controller modules to achieve persistent compromise
Attackers upload modified ladder logic or function block programs that alter the physical process being controlled by the PLC
The hardcoded cryptographic key can be extracted from Studio 5000 Logix Designer software installation directories or from memory
Attackers can disable safety alarms and modify setpoints to cause physical damage to industrial equipment or endanger personnel

detection_guidance.summary: Detection requires monitoring EtherNet/IP traffic to PLC ports for connections from unexpected source addresses, enabling controller change logging, and monitoring engineering workstations for key extraction attempts. Since no patch exists, detection and network segmentation are the primary defensive measures.

log_sources.description: Monitor all TCP/UDP connections to PLC controllers on port 2222. Any connection from a source IP outside the authorized engineering workstation subnet is potentially malicious

Enable Change Detection (Logix Designer v20+) and controller change logs (v17+). Monitor for program uploads, downloads, firmware changes, and parameter modifications not correlated with planned maintenance windows
Deploy OT-aware monitoring (Claroty, Dragos, Armis, etc.) to establish baseline EtherNet/IP communication patterns and alert on anomalies including authentication attempts from new sources
Monitor Studio 5000 / RSLogix 5000 process activity for unusual network connections, key file access, or memory inspection activity that may indicate key extraction attempts

what_to_look_for.indicator: EtherNet/IP connection from non-whitelisted source IP
what_to_look_for.description: Any controller authentication on port 2222 from an IP address not on the approved engineering workstation allowlist indicates potential CVE-2021-22681 exploitation
what_to_look_for.log_field: dst.port=2222 AND src.ip NOT IN engineering_workstation_allowlist

what_to_look_for.indicator: Controller program upload/download outside maintenance window
what_to_look_for.description: PLC code modifications occurring outside approved change windows, especially from new source addresses, are a strong indicator of unauthorized access
what_to_look_for.log_field: controller.event='ProgramUpload' OR controller.event='ProgramDownload' AND time NOT IN maintenance_window

what_to_look_for.indicator: Controller mode switch to Program mode unexpectedly
what_to_look_for.description: A controller transitioning to 'Program' mode outside of scheduled maintenance enables code modification and may indicate an attack in progress

what_to_look_for.indicator: Studio 5000 accessing unusual file paths or network destinations
what_to_look_for.description: Engineering software accessing directories that may contain the cryptographic key, or making outbound connections to unexpected destinations, may indicate key extraction or exfiltration
what_to_look_for.log_field: process.name='Studio5000' AND (file.path CONTAINS 'crypto' OR net.dst.ip NOT IN expected_plc_ips)

immediate_actions.action: Network Isolation - Firewall All PLC Controllers
immediate_actions.description: Immediately implement or verify network segmentation isolating all Logix PLC controllers from internet access and untrusted IT network segments. Use industrial firewalls with stateful inspection and allowlisting. Only authorized engineering workstations should reach controller port 2222 (TCP/UDP). This is the primary mitigation as no code patch exists.

immediate_actions.action: Enable CIP Security on All Controllers
immediate_actions.description: Deploy CIP Security (Common Industrial Protocol Security) which replaces the vulnerable hardcoded key mechanism with TLS/DTLS-based mutual authentication. Requires CIP Security-capable modules (e.g., 1756-EN4TR for ControlLogix). This is the only remediation that eliminates the root cause vulnerability.

immediate_actions.action: Set Controller Mode to 'Run' When Not Programming
immediate_actions.description: Configure all controllers to 'Run' mode using the physical key switch when active development is not in progress. Run mode prevents unauthorized program uploads without physical keyswitch access, adding a physical security barrier against remote exploitation.

immediate_actions.action: Enable Controller Change Logging and Alerting
immediate_actions.description: Enable the Change Detection feature in Logix Designer (v20+) and configure change log alerts. Integrate alerts into SIEM for rapid detection of unauthorized PLC modifications. Establish baselines of normal engineering activity to enable anomaly detection.

immediate_actions.action: Audit and Restrict Engineering Workstation Access
immediate_actions.description: Limit Studio 5000 / RSLogix 5000 installation to authorized personnel only. Implement MFA for remote access to engineering environments. Disable remote desktop access to engineering workstations if not operationally required. Verify no workstations are internet-exposed.
